speaker: interviewer
text: --2. The interviewee is Phyllis Cooper Young. Today is November 6, 1995. Phyllis, we were talking about the night that-- or the morning that you were taken. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us-- on the way they picked up some other girls that we knew, and they took us to a place in the city. I don't know. It must have been a warehouse, a huge, huge warehouse. And they were bringing them in and in, and then we were about 90. I think-- I'm almost positive that we-- I-- I have the distinction-- if you want to call it this-- of being the first forced slave, forced labor camp, for Jewish women. They brought us in. They--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you, what were you permitted to take with your?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Scared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because we didn't know where we were going. Didn't even hear of Auschwitz yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents do when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing they could do. There was just absolutely nothing. What are they going to-- we were just like-- I really even shouldn't say that because this was the whole thing of the whole Holocaust. It's like, we're going to-- to slaughter you, kid. There's nothing you can say. And nobody's going to listen to you, and you got to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long were you in this warehouse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they fed us. If you want to call it food, they fed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: On-- they didn't have-- I don't think they had any bunks. I don't remember. I think on just sacks with straw or something on the floor, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was what they called a durchgangslager.

speaker: interviewer
text: Durchgangslager.

speaker: interviewee
text: --gangslager.

speaker: interviewer
text: A transit place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then on November the 7th at 5 o'clock in the morning, they lined us up. By then, there were about 90 of us. And also, they not only came to the house, but they picked some of them up right from the street. And they took us to a-- to the train, the station. They put us on a train. Believe it or not, a passenger train.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they take you to the station?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walking. We were like four in a row, like in a column.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of the citizens of the city see you walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was too early in the morning, 5 o'clock in the morning. And they put us on a train. Nobody told us where we're going. We didn't know where we're going. And I think we were on the train for maybe 15 hours, 20 hours. And we started coming into a mountainous, beautiful, beautiful country. And it had snowed already, you know, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We still didn't know where we were going, till we arrived at the destination, which was Sudetenland, that Hitler took away from Czechoslovakia in 1938. Sudetenland-- that particular area was famous for textile factories. Every few blocks you had another factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a city that you were taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- there was a bigger city not far from there, but we weren't in the city. We were in a-- in a place, a smaller place, that was outside of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: German or Czech? Here we go again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whatever--

speaker: interviewee
text: Parschnitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now is that German or Czech?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's German.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. How do you spell Parschnitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-A-R-S-C-H-N-I-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would it be pronounced in Czech?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardubice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardubice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the bigger town next to it was Trautenau in German and Trutnov in Czech.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see when you arrived off of the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We saw something that looked like a factory. They brought us in to a-- everything was warehouses. But this particular one they brought us in was like a-- a long one, long and narrow. And it was like divided into two rooms or something like this. I can't remember. And it had the bunks set up.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many bunks in a row were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember, can't. We were 90, about 90 of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In one room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was one, but it was like sort of had a little division, which made it like two rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many--

speaker: interviewee
text: The commandant was a woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't remember. I forgot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what was she? Was she a-- a member of the German Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. She was a civilian. She was a civilian. And believe it or not, she was a pretty decent human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she Christian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She was a pretty decent human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: In what way? She told us-- you know, she explained to us what's happening here, that they need us to work. Because, like I said, he took all his men that he could to fight. The women had to replace some of the men's jobs. So we were the labor. And this-- this is a-- this was a textile factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sleep in the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was on the premises, but the factory was a little bit away. And this-- this warehouses-- it was all like you just crossed over, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a fence around this area? Were you fenced in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a fence was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not barbed wire. It was a wire, but not barbed wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not at the time. They took us in, and they-- I don't know how they decided who's going to work where, because there were various departments, I've told you once before. They started out from the raw material that textiles are made. This is called flax. They were getting a lot of it from Russia, where they occupied-- when they occupied. And so some girls were assigned to the first machines. Then the machine went-- this went to a process to the next machine, and it went through all these processes. By the time we-- our factory finished with it, it was thread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thread. All right. Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Which went to another factory, I guess, to make fabric.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fabric. Did you work at just one kind of machine, or did you do--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --different things?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, one. They assigned me to two machines. By the time I got-- the-- the stuff got to me, it was like-- it really looked like a ribbon. It was like hair and about this wide, about this wide.

speaker: interviewer
text: About an inch and a half or so?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And it was like-- it was like silky too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't even know what I should tell you what I used-- what I used to do with it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and life wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with it?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to tell you? About-- I would say about 90% of all the girls that I was with-- we were anywhere from 15 to about 25 years of age-- lost their period. I wasn't that lucky. I had it throughout the entire stay. Now, how does one protect herself?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No sanitary napkins, believe me. No rags, no nothing. I used that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you sneak it out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well then I-- at the beginning I didn't have the problem, because, like I said, she was a pretty decent-- she would even go into the drug store, and she would buy it for us. She was a pretty decent woman. But things changed. And what happened was we-- I'm-- really, I can't understand how we all learned so quickly. And we were taught by workers, you know, who worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else worked in that factory with you, besides these 90 girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people, the German people. It was a huge factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Civilians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Civilians, yeah, civilians--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: --at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards in that factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. There was like a-- what do you call-- a-- a foreman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone try to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. Not at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Later?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll come to that then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, you will.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. How did the civilians treat these 90 girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they share food with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, a couple. They were-- they were afraid. Although like-- all right, once in a while, even in-- in the-- in the good day, the first few months, which were really not bad, they-- they would come around, the-- the Gestapo, the SS, to inspect. So, you know, everybody had to be-- even the commandant, the woman, she--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a quota of what you had to produce?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mmm, no. But they used to walk around to make sure that we didn't goof off, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened if you goofed off?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, so they yelled.

speaker: interviewer
text: They yelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they yelled. Because they were still teaching us. I mean, they-- they-- they came-- they brought people who never even saw a factory like this. Even they were able to understand this, that they were teaching us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk about a typical day in your life at that factory in the beginning. When did you get up in the morning?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't know. I think 6 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to work. Oh, got--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: --breakfast, a piece of bread and some coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have appell, [? zahlappell? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you walked to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of clothing did you have? It was getting cold. Did you have a coat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, we had a coat that we came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wore one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But later on, we were given sort of a-- sort of a uniform too. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Like I said, that wasn't-- you know, just the fact that you were away from home. And like you can see the mail. We were still getting mail, which--

speaker: interviewer
text: You could hear from your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- could they send you packages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. We were able at that time to get packages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until when?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things start to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, OK. At the beginning or middle of '42, can't remember exactly when, we were called in from the job, from work. Stop your machines. They want you. We walk into a warehouse-- empty warehouse with the stone floor, and there was a big, long table set up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were about a dozen SS, Gestapo, in uniforms-- the black uniforms, the green uniforms, the swastikas. And we are told to get undressed, naked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] About-- oh, I don't know, four feet from the table where they sat, they drew a circle with chalk on the-- on the floor, and they called us, I think, alphabetically. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can I get up? No. No, you cannot.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. And they made us stand, face them, look at them, turn around. This is all naked with not a stitch of clothes on you, turn around. And they say-- they send us aside. That-- that was supposed to be a physical. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then just by looking at us, they decided who is going to stay here and work and who is going to go to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: They made a selection.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was their kind of selection. And that's when they gave us numbers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: But not--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not the tattoo.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Never had a tattoo.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you wear your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was just on a little plastic-- no, it was like a-- a little cardboard thing, a round circle, a little cardboard thing with the number. And then so we put a little plastic over it because it wouldn't have lasted very long. I wore it on a string on my neck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: 22243. But we were never tattooed. From then on, things weren't going that well anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think she was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the commandant. Replaced.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Civilian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, still civilian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, you know, we Jews, we can only survive because we have a sense of humor. We tried, ourselves, between us, you know, when nobody was looking, so to speak, to-- we put on little-- some plays. We took a-- I don't know if you ever-- you must have heard the song. We took the melody, and we put-- in-- in German-- we had the chutzpah. And the-- I remember the last line. The last line, we said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. We had the-- I mean, we didn't sing it to them, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: How else did the young women-- what else did they do to keep their morale up?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was there to do? We got up in the morning. We went to work. We came back. We [INAUDIBLE] had an incident. One of the girls, who-- she was taken with her sister and she-- very Orthodox, from what I understand. And believe it or not, she was pregnant. She didn't tell anybody. She did not. And so she kept saying she's putting on weight. How you put on weight in a concentration camp, I-- I don't know. But she was at the machine working, and she went into labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took her into the hospital. She had the baby, and they shipped her back to--

speaker: interviewer
text: To where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To home. It was to, I think, to my city. But she went to Auschwitz, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to the infant?

speaker: interviewee
text: They shipped her and the infant.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the infant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Let's talk about conditions in the camp. Tell me about the sanitary conditions.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was strictly up to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bathroom in your barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had a-- no, I think we had an outhouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you free to use it whenever you chose to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in this-- this part. But you see, what happened-- again this is-- this is not where I stayed all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Well, let's talk about this particular place. And again, what was the name of this particular place that you are talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory?

speaker: interviewer
text: The factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Walzel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. W-A-L-Z-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was the name of the textile factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: The owner's name was Walzel, and they called it Walzel.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. What about the food? What kind of food did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the beginning, for a couple of months they had a cafeteria in the factory for the workers. We were given their food--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the same--

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --food that the workers got.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. But then that-- that also changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- they sort of set up a kitchen in a-- in the-- right in the camp for us. And-- and one of the girls was-- became a cook, you know, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many meals a day were you given?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your food consist of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, when we ate with the same food that the Germans ate. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it was still the same food.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Then it was good.

speaker: interviewer
text: But afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Afterward, soup, watery soup. And if we were lucky, we'd get a piece of meat sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you, did the women try to observe any holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a-- we tried. We had a-- to show you what a human being that-- that German woman was, we had a woman who was-- had a husband who was a rabbi, an Orthodox rabbi. And she wouldn't go to work Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewer
text: On Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I guess this German woman, she must have, you know, known some about-- about Judaism. She wouldn't eat. And she was so thin that I can't begin to tell you, because she wouldn't do or eat a lot of stuff because it was treif.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't kosher.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. But you know that she told her, that Yom Kippur, she says, don't go in. I'll-- I'll-- I'll say you're sick. Stay in-- stay on-- stay in the camp. Don't go into work. That's something to be believed. There were a few of them. There were.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Tell me about disease and health. What was the health like? And-- and what happened if you did get sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I got to go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because everything that I am telling you now-- this was like-- like you would call a resort almost.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So mid-19--

speaker: interviewee
text: By the 1943, we were transferred. You see, that part of Sudeten, every couple of miles there was a factory. There was a factory that was call-- oh, oh, oh, I forgot to tell you that we weren't the original 90, because they kept sending more girl--

speaker: interviewer
text: There were more selections made?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. We used to get transports come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did more people come into--

speaker: interviewee
text: To us, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: To you also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. And then it also already was getting harder, because even though she was good to us, but as the-- as it was getting bigger, she had to have an assistant, and she had to be more careful, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there only women that were coming in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only women, only women. I never saw a Jewish man till 1945. We'll get to that, too. This Haase, which is spelled H-A-A-S-E, was another textile factory. They started a few months later than we did. And they had a woman, just like ours was good, that's how miserable she was, a real, real Nazi, Jew-hater. And they transferred us there. They took us out-- not out of the factory. We still worked in the Walzel factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back there every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far do you think it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't even judge it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you to walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasn't too far, maybe 20 minutes or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: 20 minutes?

speaker: interviewee
text: But when we were transferred there, we were now in a concentration camp. And that was a hell of a difference.

speaker: interviewer
text: Haase.

speaker: interviewee
text: Haase.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did this place look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huge. Much bigger than ours. A few levels of living quarters, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a fence around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barbed wire.

speaker: interviewer
text: Barbed wire. Was it electrified?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, women. And so help me, good God, they're worse than men.

speaker: interviewer
text: SS women?

speaker: interviewee
text: SS women in uniform. And they walked us to and from work. And they would search us to make sure-- that's why I said I don't know how I got away with that smuggling that thing for my period. They would search us, if they had any suspicion, I suppose. And the war wasn't going, so Hitler was in already fighting to France, you know, United States and Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to get mail in Haase?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Everything was cut off. No more, no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you taken to Haase?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943, in the middle of 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Middle of '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that this was a punishment for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- do you remember Heydrich-- Henry-- Heinrich-- Heydrich? He was a big, big-- Hitler's--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was--

speaker: interviewee
text: --deputy or something?

speaker: interviewer
text: --murdered.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was assassinated. And he's the one that wiped out Lidice, that-- the city in Czechoslovakia. I think this was our punishment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you told this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was our punishment, yeah. And that's when all hell broke loose. That's when-- that's when we hurt really. And you know something? The funny thing-- this-- I swear, I don't know how people's mind-- she was better to her own girls than she was to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by her own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because while-- because she had the type of camp that we had in the beginning. She got a group of girls that came in maybe three or four months later than we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of Jewish girls, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, Jewish girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she treated--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a hell of a lot. But-- but she-- she was inclined because she knew them. See, we came in on her [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: But was there ever a time when the whole group was punished for the acts of one person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to witness some hangings. People tried to escape. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they successful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, nobody was successful. Nobody was successful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They hung them. And we had to watch so we wouldn't do it. We had to bury a few of the girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually participate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I participated in the burial. And I'll never forget this because dead bodies are very heavy. Although, they-- you couldn't be heavy. But-- and we had her in a sack, and the four of us-- I mean, we-- we, ourselves, were weak, you know. I mean, well, what-- to survive on what they gave you. And she was so heavy and dead that we-- and we could only hold her by the fabric of the sack. Then when we let-- we just couldn't hold it, and we let go, and she fell down. She fell in face-down. And we knew that the Jewish religion, you don't bury face-down. So we stood there, four of us, and we struggled and struggled with the-- the sack and stuff and finally managed to get her face-up and bury her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of this woman--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you buried? Why was she killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think she was killed. I think she died. She died. Then they would have-- they would come in, and they would-- I don't know, they call it inspection. I don't know. There was one girl who was pregnant--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she--

speaker: interviewee
text: --visibly. She was brought into the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was brought that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And that poor thing. She hid under the bed, and they got a hold of her. That was the end of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That I witness, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any contact between men and women workers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We had contact with the French prisoners of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aha. But that was in the-- well, in our factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- yes. There were French prisoners of war that worked there.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Phyllis, we're coming to the end of the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a moment.
